content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES ColvlrvussloN
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street . Sacramento. CA 958(2-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
March 30, 1999
Kate Neiswender
Senate Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife
State Capitol, Room 2080
Sacramento, California 95814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-054
Dear Ms. Neiswender:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Because you have not provided all facts material to the
question presented, we are treating this letter as a request for informal advice under Regulation
18329.2 Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any
conduct which may already have taken place.
QUESTION
Is it permissible under the Act’s conflict of interest provisions for you to continue
representing or advising clients as a land use/environmental attorney, while employed as a staff
consultant to the Senate Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife?
CONCLUSION
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions do not limit the conduct ofyour private business
while you are employed by the Senate. However, these provisions may limit your activities as a
Senate consultant.
Government Code sections 81000 -91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


File No. 1-99-054
Page 2
FACTS
On February 1, 1999, you began full time employment as a consultant to the Senate
Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife. You work on bills with environmental or land
use implications, advising lawmakers on statutory language, possible revisions, and whether
particulaf bills merit support or opposition. You also provide some assistance in shepherding
bills through the Committee. Among the bills you are currently reviewing is one which may
effect the law under CEQA. You are also helping Senator Hayden with a bill prescribing internal
reforms at the Metropolitan Water District (“MWD”).
We do not have enough information on
either of these bills to enable us to gauge the foreseeable effects of any resulting legislation.
As an attorney, you have operated since 1991 as a sole practitioner specializing in
environmental and land use law. You would like to continue your professional relationships with
several clients. One ofthese is an unidentified person or entity that has a business in resources
extraction and land development. You advise this client on a wide variety of issues, including
existing law and practice under state and federal Endangered Species Acts, the California
Environmental Quality Act (“CEQA”), and the Planning and Zoning Law.
A second client is a person or entity suing two local public entities under CEQA, the
Coastal Act, and the Planning and Zoning Law. The matter is active and pending in Ventura
County Superior Court.
Because of your work in the Senate, someone else will step in as lead
counsel. You would like to be able, however, to continue providing advice to this client.
Your third client is the San Bernardino Chapter ofthe Audubon Society, which is
contesting (under CEQA and the California Endangered Species Act) MWD’s creation of a
mitigation bank at Lake Mathews. You handled the case at trial and subsequent appellate
proceedings. The remaining step is oral argument at the appellate court, to be followed by a
motion for attorney’s fees under CCP Section 1021.5, if you are successful on appeal. This case
is pro bono, but you expect to be paid ifyou prevail on the attorney’s fee motion.
The factual information you have furnished is sufficient to give us an understanding of
the general nature ofyour concerns, although we can only speculate as to foreseeable effects on
your clients ofthe bills you have described. We respond by outlining the legal framework into
which you may introduce the necessary facts to reach, through your own analysis, conclusions
appropriate to specific circumstances.


File No. 1-99-054
Page 3
ANALYSIS
The Act’s conflict of interest provisions help to insure that public officials perform their
duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those ofpersons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public
official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
3
A public official has a “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within the meaning
of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
one or more of the public official’s economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
The Commission has adopted an ordered process for determining whether the Act’s conflict of
interest restrictions apply to a given public official with regard to a particular governmental
decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)
At the outset, it is important to emphasize that the Act does not forbid any person from
holding any position or employment, public or private. The Act’s conflict of interest rules apply
only to limit the participation of a public official in governmental decisionmaking, when the
decision will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the official’s economic interest(s).
4
With this in mind, we proceed through the required steps of conflicts analysis under the Act.
A. Confirming your status as a public official
The conflict of interest provisions ofthe Act apply only to “public officials.” A “public
official” is defined to include “every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency ....“ (Section 82048.) As an employee ofthe state Senate, you are clearly a
“public official” within the meaning of the Act.
Certain public officials, including employees ofthe Legislature, are also governed by Section 87407,
which prohibits participation in decisiomnaking that may influence the official’s prospective employment in the
private sector. Regulation 18747 (copy enclosed) describes the conduct prohibited under Section 87407. In
addition to the “conventional” conflicts analysis discussed in the body of this letter, you must be careful to avoid
conduct potentially influencing your future employment.
In addition to the Act, there are other bodies of law potentially applicable to the questions raised in your
letter, such as Government Code Section 1090, the Public Contracts Code, Codes of Ethics applicable to Senate
employees, the law of incompatible activities, and the common law. The FPPC can offer advice only on the narrow
requirements of Political Reform Act


File No. 1-99-054
Page 4
B. Your participation in governmental decisionmaking
The Act’s conflict of interest provisibns come into play only when a public official
makes, participates in making, or in some way attempts to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows — or has reason to know — that
he or she’has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Commission regulations describe in detail
what constitutes making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
(Regulations 18702.1, 18702.2, and 18702.3, respectively.) The nature ofyour employment
suggests that you will most frequently be participating in, or influencing, governmental decisions
ultimately “made” by the elected officials you advise.
5
A public official “participates in making a governmental decision,” when, acting within
the authority of his or her position, the official does any ofthe following:
•
negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding a governmental decision; or
•
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official and the
purpose ofwhich is to influence a governmental decision, or by preparing or presenting
any report, analysis, or opinion, orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part ofthe official and the purpose ofwhich is to influence a
governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.2(a), (b).)
As to “influencing” (when the decision is within or before the official’s own agency, as
here, where the “decisions” you describe are Senate legislative acts); “... the official is attempting
to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).)
Regulation 18702.4 contains exceptions to the regulatory definitions ofmaking,
participating in making, and influencing governmental decisions, but the matters you have
described to us do not appear to trigger any of these “exceptions.” For purposes of this analysis
we will presume that you are participating in (or influencing) governmental decisions to alter or
amend CEQA (or bodies of law ancillary to CEQA), and to change the rules regulating MWD.
Since, as noted, conflicts of interest are identifiable only in the context of specific decisions, we
will continue the analysis with these particular “decisions” in mind.
“Making” a decision refers to actions like voting, making appointments, etc. (Regulation 18702.1.)


File No. 1-99-054
Page 5
C. Identifying your economic interests.
It is next necessary to identify any ofyour economic interests potentially affected by the
decisions described above. The “economic interests” from which conflicts of interest may arise
are described by Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds:
•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he has a direct or
indirect investment
6 of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
•
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds anyposition ofmanagement
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
•
A public official has an economic interest in realproperty in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
•
An official has an economic interest in any source ofincome, including promised income,
totaling $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
•
A public official has an economic interest in any source ofgzfls to him or her ifthe gifts
total $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87 103(e); Regulation
18703.4);
•
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those ofhis or her immediate family — this is known as the
“personal financial effects” rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
Your concern was limited to the implications of continuing client relations, and you have
not asked us to consider other economic interests.
7 As to your clients, those that have paid you
$250 or more over the preceding 12 months are sources of income to you, within the meaning of
Section 87103(c) and Regulation 18703.3. These clients will remain “sources of income”
whether or not you continue to represent them. Ifyou cease representing any client who has
furnished income to you in the past 12 months, that former client will only cease to be a “source
of income” when 12 months have elapsed since its last payment of $250 or more.
You should note that, since “source of income” includes sources ofpromised income,
the Audubon Society may be a source of income to you, even though your representation has
beenpro bono and you have not yet been paid for your services. This client’s status under the
6 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official’s agents, spouse, or dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)
Of course your law practice, to the extent it continues in existence during your tenure as a public official,
is also a potentially disqualif’ing economic interest.
(Section 87103(a) and (d).)


File No. 1-99-054
Page 6
Act rests on the terms of your client retention agreement. If your expectation ofpayment from a
successful attorney’s fees motion is grounded in an enforceable promise by the Audubon Society
to pay you from the money it collects on ajüdgment (for attorney’s fees or otherwise), you have
an economic interest in the Audubon Society as a source ofpromised income. Similarly, if the
Audubon Society assumed some contingent liability for attorney’s fees, or is liable to you for
litigation’costs or other expenses, it may be a source ofpromised income for these reasons.
You have not identified MWD as one of your past or present fee-paying clients, and you
report no promise by MWD relating to future payments of any sort. On these facts, MWD
cannot now be placed among your economic interests. It is conceivable that MWD could in the
future become a source of income to you, particularly if the court were to enter a fee judgment
listing you as the judgment creditor. But prior to entry of any judgment a litigation adversary, as
such, will not be regarded as a source of income under the Act.
You should bear in mind that a public official always has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances (i.e., personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities), and those ofhis or
her immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) This is commonly called the
“personal financial effect” rule. You have not, however, reported any facts tending to suggest
that the decisions in which you may be involved would have a personal financial effect on you,
separate and apart from any possible effects on the other economic interests listed above.
Your questions were limited to the potential for conflicts of interest inherent in your
participation in two “decisions:” possible amendments to CEQA, and regulation ofMWD. The
economic interests you have identified to us includes only certain fee-paying clients of your law
practice and, possibly, the Audubon Society.
Having identified the decisions at issue, and the
economic interests possibly involved, we are ready for the next step in the analysis.
D. Determining whether your economic interests are directly or indirectly involved
in your governmental decisions.
The next step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest is to detennine whether the
official’s interests are directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision(s) at issue.
(Regulation 18700(b)(4).)
You have an economic interest in your clients because they are
sources of income. A source of income is directly involved in a decision when it:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.l(a)(l)); or
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704. l(a)(2).)


File No. 1-99-054
Page 7
For any particular decision, if either of the preceding criteria applies to a source of
income, it is regarded as directly involved in the decision. In all other cases the source of income
is deemed indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(b); seealso Johannessen
Advice Letter, No. 1-94-138, on legislation involving rules of general application.) The limited
facts you have provided to us suggest that none of your sources of income are directly involved
in decisiOns relating to amendment of CEQA, or in decisions on regulation ofMWD.
E. Applying the materiality standard and determining the foreseeability of material
financial effect.
Regulation 18705.3(b) prescribes materiality standards used when an official’s source of
income is indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
Subdivisions (b)(1)-(b)(3) apply,
respectively, to sources of income which are business entities, non-profit entities, or natural
persons. Under the first two subdivisions the materiality standard varies with the financial
resources of the entities at issue, so for business entities and nonprofits it is necessary to know
specific financial information before the materiality standard can be identified. As to natural
persons, an effect is material if it will affect the individual’s income, investments, or other assets
or liabilities, in an amount of $1,000 or more, or if it affects the individual’s real property assets
to a degree deemed material for public officials under Regulation 18705 .2(b) or (c).
You have not provided us with information on the legal structure or size of your clients,
so we cannot identify the precise materiality standard applicable to each. Given the requisite
information, you can readily select the appropriate standards yourself.
When a source of income is indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the existence
of a conflict of interest depends on whether it is “reasonably foreseeable” (defined at Regulation
18706 as “substantially likely”) that the decision will result in a financial effect equal to or
exceeding the level set by the materiality standard applicable to the economic interest in
question. If the answer is “yes,” the official will have a conflict ofinterest (unless the public
generally exception applies). If the answer is “no,” there is no conflict of interest under the Act.
The foreseeability of material financial effects on economic interests is an intensely
factual question that can only be resolved through the exercise of soundjudgment, with due
regard for the context of material facts and surrounding circumstances. It is the responsibility of
the public official to take reasonable measures to inform himself of the facts necessary to the
decision. Generally, the Commission is in no position to answer such fact-dependant questions..
In the present case, certainly, we do not have information sufficient to determine whether it is
foreseeable that decisions on “the law under CEQA,” or on regulation ofMWD, will foreseeably
have a material financial effect on any ofyour sources of income. You will have to complete the
analysis, by application of the pertinent facts known or reasonably available to you.


File No. 1-99-054
Page 8
F. The “public generally” exception.
If, after completing the analysis to this point, you find that you have a conflict of interest
that would disqualify you from any role in a particular decision, there are nonetheless some
circumstances in which you might still be able to participate in the decision.
When it can be
shown that the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a governmental decision on the
official’s economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on “the public generally,” then the
public official is considered not to have a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18707(a).) This rule is known as the “public generally exception.”
The reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official’s financial
interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will affect a “significant segment” ofthe public “in substantially the
same manner” as it will affect the official’s economic interest. (Regulation 1 8707(b)(1), (2).)
This exception exists because of the practical difficulties in finding public officials unaffected by
a decision affecting the public generally.
We do not have enough information even to decide whether you may have a conflict of
interest in either of the decisions you have described to us. So we cannot determine whether a
hypothetical financial effect on one ofyour clients is indistinguishable from the putative effects
on “the public generally.” But whenever you conclude that you would otherwise have a
disqualifying conflict of interest, you should consider the possible application ofthis exception.
If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
aJLL
By:
Lawrence T. Woodlock
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

official’s interests are directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision(s) at issue.
(Regulation 18700(b)(4).)
You have an economic interest in your clients because they are
sources of income. A source of income is directly involved in a decision when it:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.l(a)(l)); or
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A source of income is the subject of a proceeding if a
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.
(Regulation 18704. l(a)(2).)
For any particular decision, if either of the preceding criteria applies to a source of
income, it is regarded as directly involved in the decision. In all other cases the source of income
is deemed indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(b); seealso Johannessen
Advice Letter, No. 1-94-138, on legislation involving rules of general application.) The limited
facts you have provided to us suggest that none of your sources of income are directly involved
in decisiOns relating to amendment of CEQA, or in decisions on regulation ofMWD.
E. Applying the materiality standard and determining the foreseeability of material
financial effect.
Regulation 18705.3(b) prescribes materiality standards used when an official’s source of
income is indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
Subdivisions (b)(1)-(b)(3) apply,
respectively, to sources of income which are business entities, non-profit entities, or natural
persons. Under the first two subdivisions the materiality standard varies with the financial
resources of the entities at issue, so for business entities and nonprofits it is necessary to know
specific financial information before the materiality standard can be identified. As to natural
persons, an effect is material if it will affect the individual’s income, investments, or other assets
or liabilities, in an amount of $1,000 or more, or if it affects the individual’s real property assets
to a degree deemed material for public officials under Regulation 18705 .2(b) or (c).
You have not provided us with information on the legal structure or size of your clients,
so we cannot identify the precise materiality standard applicable to each. Given the requisite
information, you can readily select the appropriate standards yourself.
When a source of income is indirectly involved in a governmental decision, the existence
of a conflict of interest depends on whether it is “reasonably foreseeable” (defined at Regulation
18706 as “substantially likely”) that the decision will result in a financial effect equal to or
exceeding the level set by the materiality standard applicable to the economic interest in
question. If the answer is “yes,” the official will have a conflict ofinterest (unless the public
generally exception applies). If the answer is “no,” there is no conflict of interest under the Act.
The foreseeability of material financial effects on economic interests is an intensely
factual question that can only be resolved through the exercise of soundjudgment, with due
regard for the context of material facts and surrounding circumstances. It is the responsibility of
the public official to take reasonable measures to inform himself of the facts necessary to the
decision. Generally, the Commission is in no position to answer such fact-dependant questions..
In the present case, certainly, we do not have information sufficient to determine whether it is
foreseeable that decisions on “the law under CEQA,” or on regulation ofMWD, will foreseeably
have a material financial effect on any ofyour sources of income. You will have to complete the
analysis, by application of the pertinent facts known or reasonably available to you.
F. The “public generally” exception.
If, after completing the analysis to this point, you find that you have a conflict of interest
that would disqualify you from any role in a particular decision, there are nonetheless some
circumstances in which you might still be able to participate in the decision.
When it can be
shown that the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a governmental decision on the
official’s economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on “the public generally,” then the
public official is considered not to have a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18707(a).) This rule is known as the “public generally exception.”
The reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official’s financial
interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will affect a “significant segment” ofthe public “in substantially the
same manner” as it will affect the official’s economic interest. (Regulation 1 8707(b)(1), (2).)
This exception exists because of the practical difficulties in finding public officials unaffected by
a decision affecting the public generally.
We do not have enough information even to decide whether you may have a conflict of
interest in either of the decisions you have described to us. So we cannot determine whether a
hypothetical financial effect on one ofyour clients is indistinguishable from the putative effects
on “the public generally.” But whenever you conclude that you would otherwise have a
disqualifying conflict of interest, you should consider the possible application ofthis exception.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is it permissible under the Act’s conflict of interest provisions for you to continue
representing or advising clients as a land use/environmental attorney, while employed as a staff
consultant to the Senate Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife?

CONCLUSION: The Act’s conflict of interest provisions do not limit the conduct ofyour private business
while you are employed by the Senate. However, these provisions may limit your activities as a
Senate consultant.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES ColvlrvussloN P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramento. CA 958(2-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 30, 1999 Kate Neiswender Senate Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife State Capitol, Room 2080 Sacramento, California 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. 1-99-054 Dear Ms. Neiswender: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Because you have not provided all facts material to the question presented, we are treating this letter as a request for informal advice under Regulation 18329.2 Please bear in mind that nothing in this letter should be construed as evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place. QUESTION Is it permissible under the Act’s conflict of interest provisions for you to continue representing or advising clients as a land use/environmental attorney, while employed as a staff consultant to the Senate Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife? CONCLUSION The Act’s conflict of interest provisions do not limit the conduct ofyour private business while you are employed by the Senate. However, these provisions may limit your activities as a Senate consultant. Government Code sections 81000 -91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).) File No. 1-99-054 Page 2 FACTS On February 1, 1999, you began full time employment as a consultant to the Senate Committee on Natural Resources and Wildlife. You work on bills with environmental or land use implications, advising lawmakers on statutory language, possible revisions, and whether particulaf bills merit support or opposition. You also provide some assistance in shepherding bills through the Committee. Among the bills you are currently reviewing is one which may effect the law under CEQA. You are also helping Senator Hayden with a bill prescribing internal reforms at the Metropolitan Water District (“MWD”). We do not have enough information on either of these bills to enable us to gauge the foreseeable effects of any resulting legislation. As an attorney, you have operated since 1991 as a sole practitioner specializing in environmental and land use law. You would like to continue your professional relationships with several clients. One ofthese is an unidentified person or entity that has a business in resources extraction and land development. You advise this client on a wide variety of issues, including existing law and practice under state and federal Endangered Species Acts, the California Environmental Quality Act (“CEQA”), and the Planning and Zoning Law. A second client is a person or entity suing two local public entities under CEQA, the Coastal Act, and the Planning and Zoning Law. The matter is active and pending in Ventura County Superior Court. Because of your work in the Senate, someone else will step in as lead counsel. You would like to be able, however, to continue providing advice to this client.
